paragraphs.text: Here’s a concise, data-backed snapshot of mercury complexation drawn from NIST SRD-46. I highlight inorganic Hg(II) halide complexes, Hg(I) carboxylate-type entries, and selected organomercury (MeHg+, EtHg+) systems, with conditions where available.

claims.text: Here’s a concise, data-backed snapshot of mercury complexation

grounded_claims.evidence_snippet: Filler span: no useful database-groundable information.
Source: NIST Standard Reference Database 46 (SRD-46); tool queries in this run are against SRD-46.

paragraphs.text:
Hg(I) entries (Hg+)
SRD-46 includes Hg(I) entries with logK reported for several carboxylic acids (formic, acetic, propionic, glycolic, methoxyacetic, etc.) at 25 °C and ionic strength around 3 M, and dicarboxylates (oxalate, succinate) at 27 °C with I ≈ 2.5 M. A ΔS/ΔH set is also reported for hydrazoic acid and a polyether macrocycle. Specific numeric logK values were not exposed in the retrieved summary for these systems, but the presence of measurements across these ligand classes and conditions is confirmed.

claims.text: with logK reported for several carboxylic acids (formic, acetic, propionic, glycolic, methoxyacetic, etc.) at 25 °C and ionic strength around 3 M, and dicarboxylates (oxalate, succinate) at 27 °C with I ≈ 2.5 M.
A ΔS/ΔH set is also reported for hydrazoic acid and a polyether macrocycle.
Specific numeric logK values were not exposed in the retrieved summary for these systems
the presence of measurements across these ligand classes and conditions is confirmed.

paragraphs.text:
Organomercury cations
MeHg+ shows many entries with thiol-containing ligands (cysteine, penicillamine derivatives, glutathione, BAL, thioglycolate, mercaptoethanol, etc.) typically at 20–25 °C and I = 0.1–0.5 M; chloride and sulfide entries are also present. Selected EtHg+ halide/SCN− measurements:

claims.text: MeHg+ shows many entries with thiol-containing ligands
(cysteine, penicillamine derivatives, glutathione, BAL, thioglycolate, mercaptoethanol, etc.)
chloride and sulfide entries are also present.

paragraphs.text:
Chemistry perspective and trends
- Hg(II) shows a clear halide affinity trend: iodide complexes are significantly stronger than chloride at 25 °C (e.g., ML logK 12.87 for I− vs ~6.7–7.3 for Cl−), in line with Hg(II) behaving as a soft Lewis acid. The negative ΔH values and large positive ΔS values for Hg(II)–chloride steps are consistent with exothermic formation and substantial desolvation/ion-pairing contributions.
- Organomercury cations bind halides more weakly than Hg(II); e.g., EtHg+–Cl− ML logK 5.32 is lower than Hg(II)–Cl− ML. MeHg+ and EtHg+ show extensive complexation with thiolate-type ligands under mild conditions, reflecting the strong Hg–S affinity relevant to biochemical binding (cysteine/glutathione).

claims.text: Hg(II) shows a clear halide affinity trend
iodide complexes are significantly stronger than chloride at 25 °C
Hg(II) behaving as a soft Lewis acid
The negative ΔH values and large positive ΔS values for Hg(II)–chloride steps
Organomercury cations bind halides more weakly than Hg(II)
MeHg+ and EtHg+ show extensive complexation with thiolate-type ligands under mild conditions

Mechanistic attributions (desolvation/ion pairing) are not stated in tool results; large positive ΔS is consistent but not explicit.
EtHg+–Cl− ML logK 5.32 (25 °C, I=1) vs Hg2+–Cl− ML logK 6.72~7.3 (25 °C) ⇒ organomercury < Hg(II).
EtHg+–Cl− ML logK 5.32 < Hg2+–Cl− ML logK 6.72~7.3 (both at 25 °C).
Tool results show MeHg+ forms many thiolate complexes at 25 °C, low I; EtHg+ thiolates not listed in provided results.
Strong Hg–S affinity is established chemistry; not directly stated in tool rows.
